title: ‘President Trump,” reports NPR, “says he hasn’t decided whether he’ll launch a military strike on Iran.” This is a remar
raw_response: {"rating": 1.6, "lean": "Lean Right", "explanation": "Opinion/editorial arguing that decisions about war with Iran must come from Congress rather than unilateral presidential action. The author emphasizes constitutional originalism, legislative primacy, and limits on executive power—values aligned with the Right—even while criticizing a Republican president’s potential action. This yields a Lean Right rating. Detailed Analysis: (1) The piece grounds its argument in an originalist reading of the Constitution, asserting that the legislature holds war-making authority and that non-defensive presidential changes to foreign policy are unconstitutional—aligning with Right-coded deference to the Constitution’s original design; (2) It repeatedly frames concentrated executive power as dangerous and prefers dispersed legislative decision-making and public accountability, reflecting a limited-government, separation-of-powers emphasis associated with the Right; (3) It critiques the post–Truman trend of presidential dominance in war decisions and Congress’s acquiescence, advocating a rebalancing that reduces centralized federal power—another Right-leaning structural preference; (4) While open to the possibility that Iran may warrant extraordinary measures, it insists any shift be justified through open congressional debate rather than executive fiat, prioritizing process conservatism over interventionism, which aligns more with right-of-center constitutional restraint than with left anti-war ideology."}

parsed_output.explanation: Opinion/editorial arguing that decisions about war with Iran must come from Congress rather than unilateral presidential action. The author emphasizes constitutional originalism, legislative primacy, and limits on executive power—values aligned with the Right—even while criticizing a Republican president’s potential action. This yields a Lean Right rating. Detailed Analysis: (1) The piece grounds its argument in an originalist reading of the Constitution, asserting that the legislature holds war-making authority and that non-defensive presidential changes to foreign policy are unconstitutional—aligning with Right-coded deference to the Constitution’s original design; (2) It repeatedly frames concentrated executive power as dangerous and prefers dispersed legislative decision-making and public accountability, reflecting a limited-government, separation-of-powers emphasis associated with the Right; (3) It critiques the post–Truman trend of presidential dominance in war decisions and Congress’s acquiescence, advocating a rebalancing that reduces centralized federal power—another Right-leaning structural preference; (4) While open to the possibility that Iran may warrant extraordinary measures, it insists any shift be justified through open congressional debate rather than executive fiat, prioritizing process conservatism over interventionism, which aligns more with right-of-center constitutional restraint than with left anti-war ideology.

explanation: Opinion/editorial arguing that decisions about war with Iran must come from Congress rather than unilateral presidential action. The author emphasizes constitutional originalism, legislative primacy, and limits on executive power—values aligned with the Right—even while criticizing a Republican president’s potential action. This yields a Lean Right rating. Detailed Analysis: (1) The piece grounds its argument in an originalist reading of the Constitution, asserting that the legislature holds war-making authority and that non-defensive presidential changes to foreign policy are unconstitutional—aligning with Right-coded deference to the Constitution’s original design; (2) It repeatedly frames concentrated executive power as dangerous and prefers dispersed legislative decision-making and public accountability, reflecting a limited-government, separation-of-powers emphasis associated with the Right; (3) It critiques the post–Truman trend of presidential dominance in war decisions and Congress’s acquiescence, advocating a rebalancing that reduces centralized federal power—another Right-leaning structural preference; (4) While open to the possibility that Iran may warrant extraordinary measures, it insists any shift be justified through open congressional debate rather than executive fiat, prioritizing process conservatism over interventionism, which aligns more with right-of-center constitutional restraint than with left anti-war ideology.
